Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which body is responsible for issuing International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)?
options: The International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA)
The International Auditing and Assurance Standards Board (IAASB)
The International Public Sector Accounting Standards Board (IPSASB)
The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB, based in London, develops and publishes IFRS. The IAASB sets auditing standards, the IESBA sets ethics standards, and the IPSASB sets public sector accounting standards.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What is the role of the IFRS Foundation?
options: To prepare entity financial statements
To enforce IFRS in member countries
To oversee the IASB and fund its work
To issue IFRS
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: To oversee the IASB and fund its work
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IFRS Foundation is the oversight body that appoints the IASB, raises funds and oversees the standard-setting process. The IASB issues IFRS.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Who can adopt IFRS for their financial statements?
options: Only entities whose securities are listed or admitted to trading on a regulated exchange
Only entities operating in jurisdictions that have formally endorsed IFRS through national legislation
Only public interest entities and those preparing consolidated financial statements
Any entity; adoption is voluntary unless required by local regulation
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: Any entity; adoption is voluntary unless required by local regulation
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: IFRS can be adopted by any entity. Many jurisdictions require IFRS for listed companies or certain entities; others permit or require local GAAP.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What does IAS stand for in the context of international standards?
options: International Auditing Standard
International Assurance Standard
International Accounting Standard
International Assessment Standard
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: International Accounting Standard
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: IAS stands for International Accounting Standard. Many IAS were issued by the former IASC; the IASB has adopted them and issues new IFRS.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which of the following is a primary objective of the IASB?
options: To enforce compliance with IFRS through mandatory inspection and financial penalties for breaches
To approve national accounting standards issued by each jurisdiction's standard-setting body
To co-ordinate the external audit of entities that prepare financial statements under IFRS
To develop a single set of high-quality global accounting standards
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: To develop a single set of high-quality global accounting standards
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB's objective is to develop a single set of high-quality, understandable, enforceable and globally accepted financial reporting standards.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: When did the IASB replace the IASC?
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB replaced the International Accounting Standards Committee (IASC) in 2001. The IASB is part of the IFRS Foundation structure.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What is the relationship between IFRS and IAS?
options: IFRS apply only to SMEs; IAS apply to listed entities
IAS are superseded and no longer applicable
IAS adopted by the IASB remain in force unless replaced by IFRS
They are identical in every jurisdiction
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: IAS adopted by the IASB remain in force unless replaced by IFRS
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: Standards issued by the IASC and adopted by the IASB (IAS) remain in effect unless and until replaced or amended by the IASB (e.g. by an IFRS).

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which statement describes the applicability of IFRS to small and medium-sized entities?
options: The IASB requires all entities to apply full IFRS regardless of size or public accountability
Unlisted entities apply reduced-disclosure IFRS which omits all recognition and measurement requirements
The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard for eligible entities
SMEs follow a separate framework developed by the IESBA based on national accounting standards
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard for eligible entities
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard, a simplified set of standards for entities that do not have public accountability. Jurisdictions may permit or require its use.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What is the purpose of the IFRS Interpretations Committee?
options: To provide timely guidance on applying IFRS and to address emerging issues
The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard for eligible entities
Any entity; adoption is voluntary unless required by local regulation
To develop a single set of high-quality global accounting standards
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: To provide timely guidance on applying IFRS and to address emerging issues
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IFRS Interpretations Committee (formerly IFRIC) provides guidance on the application of IFRS and addresses issues not explicitly covered in the standards.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which body does the IFRS Foundation trustees appoint?
options: To oversee the IASB and fund its work
The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)
The IASB chair and members
International Accounting Standard
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: The IASB chair and members
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IFRS Foundation trustees appoint the IASB chair and members. The IASB is responsible for developing and issuing IFRS.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Exam practice 11: Which body is responsible for issuing International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)?
options: The International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA)
The International Auditing and Assurance Standards Board (IAASB)

The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: According to the Conceptual Framework, which of the following is a fundamental characteristic that makes financial information useful?
options: Comparability and timeliness
Relevance and faithful representation
Materiality and cost constraint
Verifiability and understandability
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Relevance and faithful representation
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The two fundamental qualitative characteristics are relevance and faithful representation. Comparability, verifiability, timeliness and understandability are enhancing characteristics.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following is an enhancing qualitative characteristic of useful financial information?
options: Materiality
Relevance
Faithful representation
Comparability
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Comparability
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Comparability, verifiability, timeliness and understandability are enhancing characteristics. Relevance and faithful representation are fundamental.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What are the two underlying assumptions in the Conceptual Framework?
options: Materiality and aggregation
Historical cost and fair value
Accrual basis and going concern
Prudence and consistency
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Accrual basis and going concern
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The Conceptual Framework identifies accrual basis and going concern as the two underlying assumptions on which financial statements are prepared.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following is a definition of an asset in the Conceptual Framework?
options: A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: An asset is a present economic resource controlled by the entity as a result of past events. An economic resource is a right that has the potential to produce economic benefits.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What is a liability under the Conceptual Framework?
options: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: A liability is a present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events. Obligations can be legal or constructive.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: When is financial information considered relevant?
options: A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Relevance means the information is capable of making a difference in user decisions. It has predictive value, confirmatory value or both, and is material.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What does faithful representation require?
options: When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Relevance and faithful representation
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Faithful representation requires completeness, neutrality and freedom from material error. It supports the fundamental characteristic of faithful representation.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following best describes the going concern assumption?
options: Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Under the going concern assumption, financial statements are prepared on the basis that the entity will continue in operation for the foreseeable future.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What is the accrual basis of accounting?
options: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Under the accrual basis, transactions and events are recognised when they occur (and meet the definition and recognition criteria), not when cash is received or paid.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following is not a main element of financial statements in the Conceptual Framework?
options: Asset
Liability
Budget
Equity
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Budget
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The main elements related to the statement of financial position are asset, liability and equity. Income and expense relate to performance. Budget is not an element.

Verifiability and understandability

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Faithful representation requires completeness, neutrality and freedom from material error. It supports the fundamental characteristic of faithful representation. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Exam practice 18: Which of the following best describes the going concern assumption?
options: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Under the going concern assumption, financial statements are prepared on the basis that the entity will continue in operation for the foreseeable future. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Exam practice 19: What is the accrual basis of accounting?
options: Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Under the accrual basis, transactions and events are recognised when they occur (and meet the definition and recognition criteria), not when cash is received or paid. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Exam practice 20: Which of the following is not a main element of financial statements in the Conceptual Framework?
options: Liability
Equity
Asset
Budget
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Budget
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The main elements related to the statement of financial position are asset, liability and equity. Income and expense relate to performance. Budget is not an element. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Revision check 21: According to the Conceptual Framework, which of the following is a fundamental characteristic that makes financial information useful? Choose the best answer.
options: Materiality and cost constraint
Comparability and timeliness
Verifiability and understandability
Relevance and faithful representation
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Relevance and faithful representation
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The two fundamental qualitative characteristics are relevance and faithful representation. Comparability, verifiability, timeliness and understandability are enhancing characteristics. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Revision check 22: Which of the following is an enhancing qualitative characteristic of useful financial information? Choose the best answer.
options: Faithful representation
Comparability
Relevance
Materiality
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Comparability
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Comparability, verifiability, timeliness and understandability are enhancing characteristics. Relevance and faithful representation are fundamental. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Revision check 23: What are the two underlying assumptions in the Conceptual Framework? Choose the best answer.
options: Prudence and consistency
Materiality and aggregation
Historical cost and fair value
Accrual basis and going concern
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Accrual basis and going concern
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The Conceptual Framework identifies accrual basis and going concern as the two underlying assumptions on which financial statements are prepared. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Revision check 24: Which of the following is a definition of an asset in the Conceptual Framework? Choose the best answer.
options: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: An asset is a present economic resource controlled by the entity as a result of past events. An economic resource is a right that has the potential to produce economic benefits. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Revision check 25: What is a liability under the Conceptual Framework? Choose the best answer.
options: A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: A liability is a present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events. Obligations can be legal or constructive. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Revision check 26: When is financial information considered relevant? Choose the best answer.
options: When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Relevance means the information is capable of making a difference in user decisions. It has predictive value, confirmatory value or both, and is material. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, which subtotals are required in the statement of profit or loss?
options: Gross profit and operating profit only
Profit before tax and profit or loss only
Operating profit and profit before tax only
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 requires specified subtotals including operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss, with defined categories to improve comparability.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What does IFRS 18 replace in respect of presentation of financial statements?
options: In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 replaces the presentation requirements of IAS 1 for the statement of profit or loss (and related guidance), introducing new categories and subtotals.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, how are operating expenses typically categorised?
options: For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
By nature or by function; management chooses
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: By nature or by function; management chooses
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: Entities may present expenses by nature (e.g. depreciation, staff costs) or by function (e.g. cost of sales, distribution). IFRS 18 specifies categories and subtotals.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What is the purpose of IFRS 18's specified categories in the statement of profit or loss?
options: To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 introduces defined categories and mandatory subtotals to improve comparability between entities and the usefulness of the statement of profit or loss.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, where is investment income from associates and joint ventures presented?
options: Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 defines categories (e.g. operating, investing, financing) and specifies where items such as share of profit of associates are presented.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Does IFRS 18 require a single statement of profit or loss or allow a two-statement approach?
options: Two statements only (profit or loss and other comprehensive income)
Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Only a combined statement
Single statement only
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 works with the overall structure of financial statements; entities may present one or two statements for profit or loss and OCI as permitted.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What is the effect of IFRS 18 on the statement of financial position?
options: It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 primarily addresses the statement of profit or loss and related presentation. The statement of financial position is still governed by IAS 1 and other standards.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, how should unusual or infrequent items be presented?
options: Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: Items are included in the appropriate category. Unusual items are not presented as a separate category; they may be disclosed in the notes if material.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: When did IFRS 18 become effective?
options: For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2025
It is not yet effective
For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 is effective for annual periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027, with earlier application permitted.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What does IFRS 18 say about management performance measures (e.g. adjusted profit)?
options: Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
They may be presented in the financial statements subject to specified disclosure and reconciliation requirements
Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: They may be presented in the financial statements subject to specified disclosure and reconciliation requirements
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 includes requirements for management-defined performance measures when presented in the financial statements, including reconciliation to GAAP subtotals.

options: Gross profit and operating profit only

In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
They may be presented in the financial statements subject to specified disclosure and reconciliation requirements
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: They may be presented in the financial statements subject to specified disclosure and reconciliation requirements
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 includes requirements for management-defined performance measures when presented in the financial statements, including reconciliation to GAAP subtotals. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 31: Under IFRS 18, which subtotals are required in the statement of profit or loss?
options: Gross profit and operating profit only
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Profit before tax and profit or loss only
Operating profit and profit before tax only
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 requires specified subtotals including operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss, with defined categories to improve comparability. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 32: What does IFRS 18 replace in respect of presentation of financial statements?
options: Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 replaces the presentation requirements of IAS 1 for the statement of profit or loss (and related guidance), introducing new categories and subtotals. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 33: Under IFRS 18, how are operating expenses typically categorised?
options: Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
By nature or by function; management chooses
For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: By nature or by function; management chooses
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: Entities may present expenses by nature (e.g. depreciation, staff costs) or by function (e.g. cost of sales, distribution). IFRS 18 specifies categories and subtotals. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 34: What is the purpose of IFRS 18's specified categories in the statement of profit or loss?
options: For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 introduces defined categories and mandatory subtotals to improve comparability between entities and the usefulness of the statement of profit or loss. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 35: Under IFRS 18, where is investment income from associates and joint ventures presented?
options: Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 defines categories (e.g. operating, investing, financing) and specifies where items such as share of profit of associates are presented. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 36: Does IFRS 18 require a single statement of profit or loss or allow a two-statement approach?
options: Two statements only (profit or loss and other comprehensive income)
Only a combined statement
Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Single statement only
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 works with the overall structure of financial statements; entities may present one or two statements for profit or loss and OCI as permitted. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: FR quick drill 37: What is the effect of IFRS 18 on the statement of financial position?
options: It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 primarily addresses the statement of profit or loss and related presentation. The statement of financial position is still governed by IAS 1 and other standards. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: When an entity changes an accounting policy, how should it be applied under IAS 8?
options: Prospectively from the date of change only
Retrospectively unless impracticable
By restating prior years only in the notes
By disclosing the effect in the current year only
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Retrospectively unless impracticable
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: IAS 8 requires retrospective application of a change in accounting policy, with restatement of prior periods, unless retrospective application is impracticable.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: How should a change in accounting estimate be accounted for under IAS 8?
options: Retrospectively by restating comparative amounts
Retrospectively unless impracticable
Yes; immaterial errors may be corrected in the current period
Prospectively in the current and future periods
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Prospectively in the current and future periods
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: A change in estimate is applied prospectively from the period of change. It does not require restatement of prior periods.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: What is a prior period error under IAS 8?
options: Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
The nature of the change, the reason, and the amount of the adjustment for each prior period and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Prior period errors are omissions from, or misstatements in, prior period financial statements arising from a failure to use reliable information that was available.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: How should material prior period errors be corrected under IAS 8?
options: Prospectively in the current and future periods
Retrospectively unless impracticable
Retrospectively by restating comparative amounts
Yes; immaterial errors may be corrected in the current period
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Retrospectively by restating comparative amounts
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Material prior period errors are corrected retrospectively in the first set of financial statements approved after discovery by restating comparative amounts.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: When may an entity change an accounting policy under IAS 8?
options: A policy change is applied retrospectively (unless impracticable); an estimate change is applied prospectively
Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: A change in policy is permitted when required by a standard/interpretation or when it provides reliable and more relevant information.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: What disclosure does IAS 8 require for a change in accounting policy?
options: An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
The nature of the change, the reason, and the amount of the adjustment for each prior period and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: The nature of the change, the reason, and the amount of the adjustment for each prior period and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: IAS 8 requires disclosure of the nature of the change, reason, and for each period presented the amount of the adjustment to each affected line item and to the opening balance of retained earnings (or other component of equity).

Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: The nature of the change, the reason, and the amount of the adjustment for each prior period and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: IAS 8 requires disclosure of the nature of the change, reason, and for each period presented the amount of the adjustment to each affected line item and to the opening balance of retained earnings (or other component of equity). This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: FR quick drill 37: What is the hierarchy of guidance when no standard applies to a transaction under IAS 8?
options: Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
A policy change is applied retrospectively (unless impracticable); an estimate change is applied prospectively
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: In making judgement, management refers to the Conceptual Framework, then to recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and accepted industry practice, in that order. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: Under IFRS 15, when should revenue from a contract with a customer be recognised?
options: When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
When the signed contract is delivered to the customer and the invoice has been raised in the accounting system
When the entity has completed all performance obligations and received full payment from the customer
When production of the goods or services is certified as complete by the entity's quality assurance team
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: IFRS 15 requires revenue to be recognised when (or as) the entity transfers control of the promised good or service to the customer.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What are the five steps in the IFRS 15 revenue recognition model?
options: Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognise revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied
Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
When the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise to transfer it is separately identifiable
An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognise revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: The five steps are: (1) identify the contract, (2) identify performance obligations, (3) determine transaction price, (4) allocate price to obligations, (5) recognise revenue when/as each obligation is satisfied.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What is a performance obligation under IFRS 15?
options: When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
A promise in a contract to transfer a good or service to the customer
At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: A promise in a contract to transfer a good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: A performance obligation is a promise in a contract to transfer a good or service (or a bundle of goods or services) that is distinct, or a series of distinct goods or services that are substantially the same.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: When is a good or service 'distinct' under IFRS 15?
options: When the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise to transfer it is separately identifiable
An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time
Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognise revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied
Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise to transfer it is separately identifiable
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: A good or service is distinct if the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the entity's promise to transfer it is separately identifiable from other promises.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What is the transaction price under IFRS 15?
options: The list price published in the entity's approved price catalogue as at the contract inception date
The total invoice price stated in the signed contract, including all applicable taxes and regulatory levies
The cash consideration actually received from the customer as at the end of the reporting period
The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: The transaction price is the amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring the promised goods or services, excluding amounts collected on behalf of third parties.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: How should variable consideration be included in the transaction price under IFRS 15?
options: When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
A promise in a contract to transfer a good or service to the customer
At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Variable consideration is estimated (using expected value or most likely amount) and included in the transaction price only to the extent that it is highly probable that a significant reversal will not occur.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: When does control of a good transfer to the customer under IFRS 15?
options: The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Control transfers when the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from the asset. This may be over time or at a point in time.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What is a contract asset under IFRS 15?
options: When the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise to transfer it is separately identifiable
An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time
Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognise revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied
Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: A contract asset is an entity's right to consideration that is conditional on something other than the passage of time (e.g. further performance). A receivable is unconditional (subject only to passage of time).

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Incremental costs of obtaining a contract (e.g. sales commission) and certain costs to fulfil a contract are capitalised as an asset and amortised or tested for impairment.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: When may an entity recognise revenue over time under IFRS 15?

An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: FR quick drill 35: What is the transaction price under IFRS 15?
options: The list price published in the entity's approved price catalogue as at the contract inception date
The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
The total invoice price stated in the signed contract, including all applicable taxes and regulatory levies
The cash consideration actually received from the customer as at the end of the reporting period
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: The transaction price is the amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring the promised goods or services, excluding amounts collected on behalf of third parties. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: FR quick drill 36: How should variable consideration be included in the transaction price under IFRS 15?
options: At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
A promise in a contract to transfer a good or service to the customer
When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Variable consideration is estimated (using expected value or most likely amount) and included in the transaction price only to the extent that it is highly probable that a significant reversal will not occur. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: FR quick drill 37: When does control of a good transfer to the customer under IFRS 15?
options: Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Control transfers when the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from the asset. This may be over time or at a point in time. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: Under IAS 2, at what amount should inventories be measured?
options: Higher of cost and net realisable value
Net realisable value only
Lower of cost and replacement cost
Lower of cost and net realisable value
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Lower of cost and net realisable value
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 2 requires inventories to be measured at the lower of cost and net realisable value. Net realisable value is estimated selling price less costs to complete and sell.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What costs may be included in the cost of inventories under IAS 2?
options: Replacement cost of inventories as at the reporting date, adjusted for normal levels of production inefficiency
Purchase price less trade discounts, rebates, and any directly attributable financing components separated under IFRS 15
Direct labour costs and variable production overheads only, excluding all fixed overheads from the cost of inventories
Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Cost includes purchase price, conversion costs (direct labour, overheads) and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition. It excludes selling costs and abnormal waste.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: Which cost formula does IAS 2 permit for interchangeable items?
options: Lower of cost and net realisable value
FIFO, weighted average, or specific identification when appropriate
No; IAS 41 applies to biological assets, though IAS 2 may apply to agricultural produce at point of harvest
Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: FIFO, weighted average, or specific identification when appropriate
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 2 permits FIFO or weighted average. Specific identification is used for items that are not interchangeable or produced for specific projects. LIFO is not permitted.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What is net realisable value under IAS 2?
options: The accounting policy, carrying amount by category, amount recognised as expense, and any write-down or reversal
Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Costs directly related to units of production (e.g. direct labour) and systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads
Estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs of completion and estimated costs to make the sale
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs of completion and estimated costs to make the sale
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: NRV is the estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less the estimated costs of completion and the estimated costs necessary to make the sale.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: How should a write-down of inventory to NRV be recognised under IAS 2?
options: Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
At fair value less costs to sell
As an expense in profit or loss
Lower of cost and net realisable value
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: As an expense in profit or loss
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: A write-down of inventory to NRV is recognised as an expense in profit or loss in the period of the write-down.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: Does IAS 2 apply to biological assets related to agricultural activity?
options: No; IAS 41 applies to biological assets, though IAS 2 may apply to agricultural produce at point of harvest
Estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs of completion and estimated costs to make the sale
The accounting policy, carrying amount by category, amount recognised as expense, and any write-down or reversal
Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: No; IAS 41 applies to biological assets, though IAS 2 may apply to agricultural produce at point of harvest
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 41 applies to biological assets (living animals or plants). Agricultural produce harvested from a biological asset is measured under IAS 2 at point of harvest (at fair value less costs to sell at that point).

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What are conversion costs in the context of IAS 2?
options: Costs directly related to units of production (e.g. direct labour) and systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads
The accounting policy, carrying amount by category, amount recognised as expense, and any write-down or reversal
Estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs of completion and estimated costs to make the sale
Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Costs directly related to units of production (e.g. direct labour) and systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Conversion costs include direct labour and a systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads that are incurred in converting materials into finished goods.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: When may inventory be stated above cost under IAS 2?
options: Lower of cost and net realisable value
At fair value less costs to sell
FIFO, weighted average, or specific identification when appropriate
Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Inventories are measured at the lower of cost and NRV. They are not stated above cost under IAS 2.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: How should inventory held by a broker-trader be measured under IAS 2?
options: Lower of cost and net realisable value
Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
As an expense in profit or loss
At fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: At fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Commodity broker-traders may measure inventories at fair value less costs to sell when this is a well-established practice. Otherwise the lower of cost and NRV applies.

options: No; IAS 41 applies to biological assets, though IAS 2 may apply to agricultural produce at point of harvest

The accounting policy, carrying amount by category, amount recognised as expense, and any write-down or reversal

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 2 requires disclosure of accounting policies, carrying amount by category, amount of inventories recognised as expense, write-downs and reversals, and other specified information.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: Under IAS 16, which of the following may be included in the cost of an item of property, plant and equipment?
options: The full invoice price paid to the supplier, including delivery, installation, and any applicable import duties
All costs incurred from the date of purchase up to the date the asset is first brought into productive use
Only the purchase price agreed with the supplier, excluding any subsequent expenditure on the asset after delivery
Directly attributable costs to bring the asset to working condition
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: Directly attributable costs to bring the asset to working condition
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: The cost of PPE includes its purchase price and any directly attributable costs to bring the asset to the location and condition necessary for it to be capable of operating in the manner intended by management.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: What measurement models does IAS 16 allow after initial recognition?
options: Cost model only
Cost model or revaluation model
Fair value model only
Fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: Cost model or revaluation model
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: After initial recognition, an entity chooses either the cost model (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment) or the revaluation model (fair value at revaluation date less subsequent depreciation and impairment).

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: How is depreciation defined under IAS 16?
options: Systematic allocation of the depreciable amount of an asset over its useful life
In other comprehensive income and accumulated in equity under revaluation surplus
When the asset is fully depreciated or when it is classified as held for sale
Directly attributable costs to bring the asset to working condition

options: Yes; each significant part with a cost that is significant in relation to the total cost of the item is depreciated separately
Recognised in profit or loss to the extent it exceeds any previous revaluation surplus for that asset

options: Yes; each significant part with a cost that is significant in relation to the total cost of the item is depreciated separately

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: What is residual value under IAS 16?
options: The estimated amount that an entity would currently obtain from disposal of the asset, after deducting estimated disposal costs, if the asset were already of the age and condition expected at the end of its useful life

The net book value of the asset at the end of its estimated useful life, calculated as original cost less total accumulated depreciation charged on a straight-line basis over the full depreciation period used by the entity

The present value of the proceeds expected to be received on disposal of the asset at the end of its useful life, discounted at the entity's pre-tax weighted average cost of capital as at the reporting date

The original purchase cost of the asset multiplied by the estimated residual percentage stated in the entity's fixed asset register at the date of initial recognition in the statement of financial position
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: The estimated amount that an entity would currently obtain from disposal of the asset, after deducting estimated disposal costs, if the asset were already of the age and condition expected at the end of its useful life
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: Residual value is the estimated amount (excluding disposal costs) that could be obtained from disposal of the asset at the end of its useful life, in its expected condition.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 16, which of the following may be included in the cost of an item of property, plant and equipment?
options: Only the purchase price agreed with the supplier, excluding any subsequent expenditure on the asset after delivery

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: Under IAS 23, when must borrowing costs be capitalised?
options: When the borrowing is directly attributable to a qualifying asset
When the total borrowing costs for the period exceed the entity's capitalisation threshold under its accounting policies
Borrowing costs are always capitalised for all assets financed by external debt regardless of the asset type
When the entity has drawn down the full loan facility and interest has started to accrue on the principal balance
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: When the borrowing is directly attributable to a qualifying asset
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: IAS 23 requires capitalisation of borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset. A qualifying asset is one that necessarily takes a substantial period to get ready for use or sale.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: What is a qualifying asset under IAS 23?
options: Any asset acquired or constructed by the entity that is financed wholly or partly by specific borrowings
A non-current asset that is subject to a finance lease and is included in the entity's statement of financial position
An asset that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale
Any asset whose cost exceeds a material threshold defined in the entity's accounting policies for capitalisation
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: An asset that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: A qualifying asset is one that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale (e.g. manufacturing plant, power generation facility, investment property).

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: Which borrowing costs are capitalised under IAS 23?
options: When expenditure on the asset, borrowing costs and activities necessary to prepare the asset for use or sale have begun
Borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset
The weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to general borrowings outstanding during the period
When substantially all the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended use or sale are complete
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: Borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: Only borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset are capitalised. Other borrowing costs are expensed.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: How is the capitalisation of borrowing costs commenced under IAS 23?
options: When substantially all the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended use or sale are complete
Borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset
When expenditure on the asset, borrowing costs and activities necessary to prepare the asset for use or sale have begun
The weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to general borrowings outstanding during the period
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: When expenditure on the asset, borrowing costs and activities necessary to prepare the asset for use or sale have begun
