Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Under IAS 20, how may a government grant related to an asset be presented in the statement of financial position?
options: As a credit directly to equity under the revaluation reserve, consistent with the IAS 16 revaluation model
Only as deferred income in the statement of financial position; deduction from the asset is not permitted under IAS 20
Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Always as a reduction in the carrying amount of the related asset with no alternative presentation permitted
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 permits two approaches: presenting the grant as deferred income (and releasing to profit or loss over the asset's life) or deducting it from the carrying amount of the asset.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: When should a government grant be recognised under IAS 20?
options: When the cash proceeds of the grant are credited to the entity's bank account and confirmed by a bank statement
When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
When the related qualifying expenditure has been incurred and approved by the entity's board of directors
When the grant application is formally submitted to the government authority and a reference number is assigned
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: A government grant is recognised when there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with the conditions attaching to it and that the grant will be received.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: How should a grant related to income be recognised under IAS 20?
options: The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Grants related to income (e.g. to subsidise operating expenses) are recognised in profit or loss over the periods in which the related costs are recognised.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: If a grant related to an asset is presented as deferred income, how is it released to profit or loss?
options: Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Only when the asset is sold or disposed of, releasing any remaining deferred income balance at that point
Immediately in full when the asset is brought into use and the grant conditions have been formally confirmed
Over the grant repayment period specified in the grant agreement rather than over the useful life of the asset
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: When the grant is presented as deferred income, it is recognised in profit or loss over the useful life of the related asset (e.g. in the same proportion as depreciation).

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: What happens if an entity fails to comply with conditions attaching to a government grant?
options: Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: If the entity does not comply with conditions, the grant may become repayable. Repayment is accounted for by revising the deferred income/asset and recognising the repayment as an expense (or reducing the asset).

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Can government grants include non-monetary assets under IAS 20?
options: No; either presentation (deduction from asset or deferred income) is permitted
When there is no reasonable assurance of compliance or receipt
Yes; they are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount
Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Yes; they are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Government grants may be in the form of non-monetary assets (e.g. land, equipment). These are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount, with the grant then accounted for in the same way as monetary grants.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: How should repayment of a grant related to an asset previously presented as a deduction from the asset be accounted for?
options: By increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent the repayment exceeds any related deferred credit
The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: By increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent the repayment exceeds any related deferred credit
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Repayment of a grant related to an asset is accounted for by increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent that the repayment exceeds any deferred credit (or the grant had been presented as a deduction from the asset).

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: What disclosure does IAS 20 require for government grants?
options: The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
By increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent the repayment exceeds any related deferred credit
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 requires disclosure of the accounting policy adopted, the nature and extent of government grants recognised in the financial statements, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies attaching to grants.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Is there a single mandatory method of presenting grants related to assets under IAS 20?
options: When there is no reasonable assurance of compliance or receipt
No; either presentation (deduction from asset or deferred income) is permitted
Yes; they are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount
Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: No; either presentation (deduction from asset or deferred income) is permitted
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 permits two approaches: (1) setting up the grant as deferred income and releasing to profit or loss over the asset's life, or (2) deducting the grant from the carrying amount of the asset.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: When might a grant not be recognised as income under IAS 20?
options: Yes; they are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount
Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
When there is no reasonable assurance of compliance or receipt
No; either presentation (deduction from asset or deferred income) is permitted
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: When there is no reasonable assurance of compliance or receipt
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: A grant is recognised only when there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with the conditions and that the grant will be received. If that assurance is lacking, the grant is not recognised until the uncertainty is resolved.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 20, how may a government grant related to an asset be presented in the statement of financial position?
options: Always as a reduction in the carrying amount of the related asset with no alternative presentation permitted
As a credit directly to equity under the revaluation reserve, consistent with the IAS 16 revaluation model
Only as deferred income in the statement of financial position; deduction from the asset is not permitted under IAS 20
Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 permits two approaches: presenting the grant as deferred income (and releasing to profit or loss over the asset's life) or deducting it from the carrying amount of the asset. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Exam practice 12: When should a government grant be recognised under IAS 20?
options: When the cash proceeds of the grant are credited to the entity's bank account and confirmed by a bank statement
When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
When the grant application is formally submitted to the government authority and a reference number is assigned
When the related qualifying expenditure has been incurred and approved by the entity's board of directors
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: A government grant is recognised when there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with the conditions attaching to it and that the grant will be received. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Exam practice 13: How should a grant related to income be recognised under IAS 20?
options: The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Grants related to income (e.g. to subsidise operating expenses) are recognised in profit or loss over the periods in which the related costs are recognised. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Exam practice 14: If a grant related to an asset is presented as deferred income, how is it released to profit or loss?
options: Over the grant repayment period specified in the grant agreement rather than over the useful life of the asset
Immediately in full when the asset is brought into use and the grant conditions have been formally confirmed
Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Only when the asset is sold or disposed of, releasing any remaining deferred income balance at that point
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: When the grant is presented as deferred income, it is recognised in profit or loss over the useful life of the related asset (e.g. in the same proportion as depreciation). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Exam practice 15: What happens if an entity fails to comply with conditions attaching to a government grant?
options: Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed

Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: If the entity does not comply with conditions, the grant may become repayable. Repayment is accounted for by revising the deferred income/asset and recognising the repayment as an expense (or reducing the asset). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Under IAS 40, which measurement model(s) may an entity use for investment property after initial recognition?
options: Either cost model or fair value model
No; it is inventory under IAS 2
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: IAS 40 allows an entity to choose either the cost model or the fair value model for all investment property. The choice is applied consistently to all such property.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: What is investment property under IAS 40?
options: Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Investment property is property (land or building, or part of a building) held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, not for use in production/supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Is property held for sale in the ordinary course of business investment property?
options: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Property held for sale in the ordinary course of business (e.g. by a property developer) is inventory under IAS 2, not investment property.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Under the fair value model in IAS 40, where are changes in fair value recognised?
options: In other comprehensive income
In equity
As a revaluation reserve
In profit or loss
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: In profit or loss
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Under the fair value model, changes in fair value of investment property are recognised in profit or loss in the period in which they arise.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: When can an entity not determine fair value reliably for investment property under IAS 40?
options: In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: If an entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis, it uses the cost model under IAS 16. This is expected to be rare (e.g. for unique property).

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Can a property be partly investment property and partly owner-occupied under IAS 40?
options: At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: If the portions could be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately (e.g. one part investment property, one part owner-occupied). If not, the property is investment property only if an insignificant portion is owner-occupied.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: How is investment property initially measured under IAS 40?
options: IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
No; it is inventory under IAS 2
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Investment property is initially measured at cost, including transaction costs. Cost is determined in accordance with the same principles as for PPE (e.g. IAS 16).

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: When does a transfer from inventory to investment property occur under IAS 40?
options: Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: A transfer from inventory to investment property is made when there is a change in use, evidenced by commencement of an operating lease to a third party. The property is reclassified and measured at fair value at the date of change.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: If an entity uses the cost model for investment property, what standard applies for measurement?
options: At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Either cost model or fair value model
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Under the cost model, investment property is measured in accordance with IAS 16: cost less accumulated depreciation and accumulated impairment losses.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: What disclosure does IAS 40 require when the fair value model is used?
options: Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: IAS 40 requires disclosure of whether cost or fair value is used, and when fair value is used: the methods and significant assumptions; and a reconciliation of the opening to closing carrying amount of investment property.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 40, which measurement model(s) may an entity use for investment property after initial recognition?
options: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: IAS 40 allows an entity to choose either the cost model or the fair value model for all investment property. The choice is applied consistently to all such property. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

options: When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Investment property is property (land or building, or part of a building) held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, not for use in production/supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 13: Is property held for sale in the ordinary course of business investment property?
options: Either cost model or fair value model
No; it is inventory under IAS 2
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Property held for sale in the ordinary course of business (e.g. by a property developer) is inventory under IAS 2, not investment property. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 14: Under the fair value model in IAS 40, where are changes in fair value recognised?
options: In equity
As a revaluation reserve
In other comprehensive income
In profit or loss
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: In profit or loss
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Under the fair value model, changes in fair value of investment property are recognised in profit or loss in the period in which they arise. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 15: When can an entity not determine fair value reliably for investment property under IAS 40?
options: When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: If an entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis, it uses the cost model under IAS 16. This is expected to be rare (e.g. for unique property). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 16: Can a property be partly investment property and partly owner-occupied under IAS 40?
options: Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: If the portions could be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately (e.g. one part investment property, one part owner-occupied). If not, the property is investment property only if an insignificant portion is owner-occupied. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 17: How is investment property initially measured under IAS 40?
options: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Investment property is initially measured at cost, including transaction costs. Cost is determined in accordance with the same principles as for PPE (e.g. IAS 16). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Exam practice 18: When does a transfer from inventory to investment property occur under IAS 40?
options: When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts

Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Under IAS 38, when may development costs be capitalised as an intangible asset?
options: Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Never; development is always expensed
When the project is technically feasible only
Whenever management intends to use the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Development costs can be capitalised only when the entity can demonstrate technical feasibility, intention and ability to complete and use or sell the asset, and that the asset will generate probable future economic benefits, among other criteria.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How are research costs treated under IAS 38?
options: Capitalised when the project is approved
Expensed when incurred
Deferred and amortised over 5 years
Capitalised when incurred
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Expensed when incurred
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Research (or the research phase of an internal project) is expensed when incurred. No intangible asset arising from research (or from the research phase) is recognised.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: What is an intangible asset under IAS 38?
options: An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance
Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset is an identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance. Identifiable means separable or arising from contractual or other legal rights.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How is the useful life of an intangible asset determined under IAS 38?
options: Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset has either a finite or an indefinite useful life. A finite life is the length of time the asset is expected to be available for use by the entity.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How is an intangible asset with an indefinite useful life measured after initial recognition under IAS 38?
options: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset with an indefinite useful life is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) and carried at cost less accumulated impairment losses.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Can intangible assets be revalued under IAS 38?
options: Only when all six recognition criteria are met
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance
Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: After initial recognition, an intangible asset may be carried at a revalued amount (fair value at revaluation date less subsequent amortisation and impairment) only if fair value can be determined by reference to an active market. Active markets for intangibles are rare.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: What is the default amortisation period for an intangible asset with a finite life when the useful life cannot be determined reliably?
options: The period is presumed to be 10 years if it cannot be determined reliably
5 years
The asset is not amortised
10 years
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: 10 years
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: IAS 38 states that the useful life of an intangible asset that is not amortised (i.e. indefinite) is reviewed each period. For finite-lived intangibles, if the useful life cannot be estimated reliably, it is presumed not to exceed 10 years in some jurisdictions; the standard requires the best estimate.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How is goodwill arising in a business combination treated under IAS 38?
options: As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Goodwill acquired in a business combination is outside the scope of IAS 38. It is accounted for under IFRS 3 (not amortised; tested for impairment annually).

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: When may an entity derecognise an intangible asset under IAS 38?
options: It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset is derecognised on disposal or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal. The gain or loss is the difference between net disposal proceeds and carrying amount.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: What disclosure does IAS 38 require for intangible assets?
options: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: IAS 38 requires disclosure of accounting policies, useful lives/amortisation rates, a reconciliation of the opening to closing carrying amount, and restrictions on title or pledged as security, among other items.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 38, when may development costs be capitalised as an intangible asset?
options: Whenever management intends to use the asset
Only when all six recognition criteria are met
When the project is technically feasible only
Never; development is always expensed
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Development costs can be capitalised only when the entity can demonstrate technical feasibility, intention and ability to complete and use or sell the asset, and that the asset will generate probable future economic benefits, among other criteria. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset has either a finite or an indefinite useful life. A finite life is the length of time the asset is expected to be available for use by the entity. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 15: How is an intangible asset with an indefinite useful life measured after initial recognition under IAS 38?
options: As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset with an indefinite useful life is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) and carried at cost less accumulated impairment losses. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 16: Can intangible assets be revalued under IAS 38?
options: An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance
Only when all six recognition criteria are met
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: After initial recognition, an intangible asset may be carried at a revalued amount (fair value at revaluation date less subsequent amortisation and impairment) only if fair value can be determined by reference to an active market. Active markets for intangibles are rare. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 17: What is the default amortisation period for an intangible asset with a finite life when the useful life cannot be determined reliably?
options: The asset is not amortised
The period is presumed to be 10 years if it cannot be determined reliably
5 years
10 years
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: 10 years
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: IAS 38 states that the useful life of an intangible asset that is not amortised (i.e. indefinite) is reviewed each period. For finite-lived intangibles, if the useful life cannot be estimated reliably, it is presumed not to exceed 10 years in some jurisdictions; the standard requires the best estimate. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 18: How is goodwill arising in a business combination treated under IAS 38?
options: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Goodwill acquired in a business combination is outside the scope of IAS 38. It is accounted for under IFRS 3 (not amortised; tested for impairment annually). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 19: When may an entity derecognise an intangible asset under IAS 38?
options: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset is derecognised on disposal or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal. The gain or loss is the difference between net disposal proceeds and carrying amount. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Exam practice 20: What disclosure does IAS 38 require for intangible assets?
options: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: IAS 38 requires disclosure of accounting policies, useful lives/amortisation rates, a reconciliation of the opening to closing carrying amount, and restrictions on title or pledged as security, among other items. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Revision check 21: Under IAS 38, when may development costs be capitalised as an intangible asset? Choose the best answer.
options: Whenever management intends to use the asset
Never; development is always expensed
When the project is technically feasible only
Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Development costs can be capitalised only when the entity can demonstrate technical feasibility, intention and ability to complete and use or sell the asset, and that the asset will generate probable future economic benefits, among other criteria. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Revision check 22: How are research costs treated under IAS 38? Choose the best answer.
options: Capitalised when the project is approved
Expensed when incurred
Deferred and amortised over 5 years
Capitalised when incurred
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Expensed when incurred
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Research (or the research phase of an internal project) is expensed when incurred. No intangible asset arising from research (or from the research phase) is recognised. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Revision check 23: What is an intangible asset under IAS 38? Choose the best answer.
options: At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised

Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Under IFRS 5, how should a non-current asset held for sale be measured?
options: Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: IFRS 5 requires non-current assets (or disposal groups) classified as held for sale to be measured at the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell. Depreciation ceases.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: When can a non-current asset (or disposal group) be classified as held for sale under IFRS 5?
options: At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Classification as held for sale requires that the asset (or disposal group) is available for immediate sale in its present condition and that the sale is highly probable (e.g. committed to a plan, active programme to locate a buyer, expected within one year).

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: What happens to depreciation when an asset is classified as held for sale?
options: It continues as before
It is charged only in the period of classification
Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
It is doubled
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: When a non-current asset (or disposal group) is classified as held for sale, the entity does not depreciate (or amortise) the asset while it is so classified.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: What is a discontinued operation under IFRS 5?
options: Any business unit that the entity's management has formally approved for closure as part of a strategic restructuring programme, regardless of whether it meets the criteria for a separate major line of business

A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
Any individual asset or cash-generating unit where the carrying amount has been determined to exceed its recoverable amount and an impairment loss has been recognised in the current reporting period

A reportable operating segment under IFRS 8 that has generated consistent operating losses over at least three consecutive reporting periods and is therefore considered unviable by the entity's management
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: A discontinued operation is a component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and (a) represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or (b) is part of a single coordinated plan to dispose of a separate major line of business or geographical area, or (c) is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: How are the results of a discontinued operation presented in the statement of profit or loss under IFRS 5?
options: As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: The post-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation and the post-tax gain or loss on disposal are presented as a single amount in the statement of profit or loss, with disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Can a disposal group include current assets under IFRS 5?
options: Current assets within a disposal group are reclassified to held for sale only if they form a separate disposal transaction
A disposal group may include non-current assets only; current assets such as inventory are excluded under IFRS 5
No; a disposal group under IFRS 5 comprises only non-current assets and does not include any current assets or liabilities
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: A disposal group is a group of assets to be disposed of, together as a group in a single transaction, and possibly liabilities directly associated with those assets. It can include current and non-current assets and liabilities.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: If the criteria for held for sale are no longer met, how should the asset (or disposal group) be accounted for under IFRS 5?
options: The asset continues to be held at its held-for-sale carrying amount and depreciation resumes from the reclassification date; no retrospective adjustment is made for depreciation that was not charged during the held-for-sale classification period

The asset is transferred to investment property and remeasured to fair value under the IAS 40 fair value model; any resulting revaluation gain is recognised immediately in profit or loss and a revised depreciation schedule is established from the transfer date

It is reclassified back to the appropriate standard (e.g. IAS 16) and measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell

The asset is remeasured to its original historical cost as though the held-for-sale classification had never been applied; any difference between the original cost and the held-for-sale carrying amount is recognised in other comprehensive income in equity
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: It is reclassified back to the appropriate standard (e.g. IAS 16) and measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: When the criteria for held for sale are no longer met, the asset (or disposal group) is reclassified. It is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation/amortisation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: What are 'costs to sell' under IFRS 5?
options: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Costs to sell are the incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal of an asset (or disposal group), excluding finance costs and income tax expense.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: When an asset is reclassified from held for sale, how is the recovery in value (above the adjusted carrying amount) treated?
options: As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
The asset is measured at the lower of the adjusted carrying amount and recoverable amount; any increase above the pre-classification carrying amount is limited by what depreciation would have been

A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: The asset is measured at the lower of the adjusted carrying amount and recoverable amount; any increase above the pre-classification carrying amount is limited by what depreciation would have been
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: On reclassification, the asset is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell. The standard does not permit carrying amount to exceed what it would have been without the held-for-sale classification.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Is a non-current asset that is to be abandoned (e.g. no sale) classified as held for sale?
options: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Assets (or disposal groups) to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale. Abandonment means the asset will be used until the end of its useful life or will be exchanged for another asset; recovery is not principally through a sale.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 11: Under IFRS 5, how should a non-current asset held for sale be measured?
options: When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: IFRS 5 requires non-current assets (or disposal groups) classified as held for sale to be measured at the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell. Depreciation ceases. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 12: When can a non-current asset (or disposal group) be classified as held for sale under IFRS 5?
options: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Classification as held for sale requires that the asset (or disposal group) is available for immediate sale in its present condition and that the sale is highly probable (e.g. committed to a plan, active programme to locate a buyer, expected within one year). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 13: What happens to depreciation when an asset is classified as held for sale?
options: Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
It is doubled
It continues as before
It is charged only in the period of classification
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: When a non-current asset (or disposal group) is classified as held for sale, the entity does not depreciate (or amortise) the asset while it is so classified. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 14: What is a discontinued operation under IFRS 5?
options: Any individual asset or cash-generating unit where the carrying amount has been determined to exceed its recoverable amount and an impairment loss has been recognised in the current reporting period

A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale

Any business unit that the entity's management has formally approved for closure as part of a strategic restructuring programme, regardless of whether it meets the criteria for a separate major line of business

A reportable operating segment under IFRS 8 that has generated consistent operating losses over at least three consecutive reporting periods and is therefore considered unviable by the entity's management
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: A discontinued operation is a component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and (a) represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or (b) is part of a single coordinated plan to dispose of a separate major line of business or geographical area, or (c) is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 15: How are the results of a discontinued operation presented in the statement of profit or loss under IFRS 5?
options: Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: The post-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation and the post-tax gain or loss on disposal are presented as a single amount in the statement of profit or loss, with disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 16: Can a disposal group include current assets under IFRS 5?
options: No; a disposal group under IFRS 5 comprises only non-current assets and does not include any current assets or liabilities
A disposal group may include non-current assets only; current assets such as inventory are excluded under IFRS 5
Current assets within a disposal group are reclassified to held for sale only if they form a separate disposal transaction
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: A disposal group is a group of assets to be disposed of, together as a group in a single transaction, and possibly liabilities directly associated with those assets. It can include current and non-current assets and liabilities. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 17: If the criteria for held for sale are no longer met, how should the asset (or disposal group) be accounted for under IFRS 5?
options: The asset continues to be held at its held-for-sale carrying amount and depreciation resumes from the reclassification date; no retrospective adjustment is made for depreciation that was not charged during the held-for-sale classification period

The asset is transferred to investment property and remeasured to fair value under the IAS 40 fair value model; any resulting revaluation gain is recognised immediately in profit or loss and a revised depreciation schedule is established from the transfer date

The asset is remeasured to its original historical cost as though the held-for-sale classification had never been applied; any difference between the original cost and the held-for-sale carrying amount is recognised in other comprehensive income in equity

It is reclassified back to the appropriate standard (e.g. IAS 16) and measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: It is reclassified back to the appropriate standard (e.g. IAS 16) and measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: When the criteria for held for sale are no longer met, the asset (or disposal group) is reclassified. It is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation/amortisation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 18: What are 'costs to sell' under IFRS 5?
options: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Costs to sell are the incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal of an asset (or disposal group), excluding finance costs and income tax expense. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 19: When an asset is reclassified from held for sale, how is the recovery in value (above the adjusted carrying amount) treated?
options: No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction

A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
The asset is measured at the lower of the adjusted carrying amount and recoverable amount; any increase above the pre-classification carrying amount is limited by what depreciation would have been
As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: The asset is measured at the lower of the adjusted carrying amount and recoverable amount; any increase above the pre-classification carrying amount is limited by what depreciation would have been
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: On reclassification, the asset is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell. The standard does not permit carrying amount to exceed what it would have been without the held-for-sale classification. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Exam practice 20: Is a non-current asset that is to be abandoned (e.g. no sale) classified as held for sale?
options: No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Assets (or disposal groups) to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale. Abandonment means the asset will be used until the end of its useful life or will be exchanged for another asset; recovery is not principally through a sale. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Revision check 21: Under IFRS 5, how should a non-current asset held for sale be measured? Choose the best answer.
options: Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: IFRS 5 requires non-current assets (or disposal groups) classified as held for sale to be measured at the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell. Depreciation ceases. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Revision check 22: When can a non-current asset (or disposal group) be classified as held for sale under IFRS 5? Choose the best answer.
options: Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Classification as held for sale requires that the asset (or disposal group) is available for immediate sale in its present condition and that the sale is highly probable (e.g. committed to a plan, active programme to locate a buyer, expected within one year). This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Revision check 23: What happens to depreciation when an asset is classified as held for sale? Choose the best answer.
options: It continues as before
It is charged only in the period of classification
It is doubled
Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Depreciation (or amortisation) ceases
