Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Under IAS 12, what is a deferred tax liability?
options: The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
The amount attributable to the asset for tax purposes
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax liability is the amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences. Taxable temporary differences result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: What is a deferred tax asset under IAS 12?
options: The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax asset is the amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, carryforward of unused tax losses and unused tax credits.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: What is a taxable temporary difference?
options: As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Taxable temporary differences are temporary differences that will result in taxable amounts in determining taxable profit (tax loss) of future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: At what rate should deferred tax be measured under IAS 12?
options: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate

At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Deferred tax is measured at the tax rates that are expected to apply when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, using tax rates (and laws) enacted or substantively enacted at the reporting date.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: When may a deferred tax asset be recognised under IAS 12?
options: The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax asset is recognised for deductible temporary differences, unused losses and unused tax credits to the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which they can be utilised.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: How is current tax defined in IAS 12?
options: A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Current tax is the amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past reporting periods.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Where is current tax and deferred tax presented in the financial statements under IAS 12?
options: A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Current and deferred tax are recognised as income or expense and included in profit or loss for the period, except to the extent that the tax arises from (a) a transaction or event recognised in OCI or directly in equity, or (b) a business combination.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Can deferred tax arise from revaluation of assets under IAS 12?
options: The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: When an asset is revalued, its carrying amount changes but its tax base generally does not, so a temporary difference arises. Deferred tax on revaluations may be recognised in OCI to the extent the revaluation is in OCI.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: What is the tax base of an asset under IAS 12?
options: The amount attributable to the asset for tax purposes
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: The amount attributable to the asset for tax purposes
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: The tax base of an asset is the amount that would be deductible for tax purposes against any taxable economic benefits that would flow to the entity on recovery of the carrying amount. If those benefits are not taxable, the tax base equals the carrying amount.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Does IAS 12 require disclosure of a reconciliation of tax expense?
options: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised

At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: IAS 12 requires an explanation of the relationship between tax expense (income) and accounting profit (e.g. a numerical reconciliation between tax expense and the product of accounting profit and the applicable tax rate, or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate).

The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: The tax base of an asset is the amount that would be deductible for tax purposes against any taxable economic benefits that would flow to the entity on recovery of the carrying amount. If those benefits are not taxable, the tax base equals the carrying amount. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Exam practice 20: Does IAS 12 require disclosure of a reconciliation of tax expense?
options: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate

At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: IAS 12 requires an explanation of the relationship between tax expense (income) and accounting profit (e.g. a numerical reconciliation between tax expense and the product of accounting profit and the applicable tax rate, or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Revision check 21: Under IAS 12, what is a deferred tax liability? Choose the best answer.
options: As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
The amount attributable to the asset for tax purposes
The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax liability is the amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences. Taxable temporary differences result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

options: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity

A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax asset is the amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, carryforward of unused tax losses and unused tax credits. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Taxable temporary differences are temporary differences that will result in taxable amounts in determining taxable profit (tax loss) of future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Revision check 24: At what rate should deferred tax be measured under IAS 12? Choose the best answer.
options: Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)

At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Deferred tax is measured at the tax rates that are expected to apply when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, using tax rates (and laws) enacted or substantively enacted at the reporting date. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Revision check 25: When may a deferred tax asset be recognised under IAS 12? Choose the best answer.
options: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Revision check 26: How is current tax defined in IAS 12? Choose the best answer.
options: A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Current tax is the amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past reporting periods. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Revision check 27: Where is current tax and deferred tax presented in the financial statements under IAS 12? Choose the best answer.
options: As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Current and deferred tax are recognised as income or expense and included in profit or loss for the period, except to the extent that the tax arises from (a) a transaction or event recognised in OCI or directly in equity, or (b) a business combination. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Revision check 28: Can deferred tax arise from revaluation of assets under IAS 12? Choose the best answer.
options: To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.correct: Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: When an asset is revalued, its carrying amount changes but its tax base generally does not, so a temporary difference arises. Deferred tax on revaluations may be recognised in OCI to the extent the revaluation is in OCI. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Under IFRS 9, how are debt instruments measured when held in the amortised cost category?
options: At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial assets measured at amortised cost are initially recognised at fair value and subsequently at amortised cost using the effective interest method, less any impairment. The business model must be to hold to collect contractual cash flows.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: What are the three main classification categories for financial assets under IFRS 9?
options: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Under IFRS 9, financial assets are classified as measured at amortised cost, at fair value through other comprehensive income, or at fair value through profit or loss, depending on the business model and the contractual cash flow characteristics.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: When can a debt instrument be measured at amortised cost under IFRS 9?
options: A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount

When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding

When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is measured at amortised cost if (1) it is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and (2) its contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest (SPPI).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: What is the effective interest method under IFRS 9?
options: When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)

When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding

A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: The effective interest method is a method of calculating the amortised cost of a financial asset or liability and of allocating the interest income or expense over the relevant period. The effective interest rate is the rate that exactly discounts estimated future cash flows to the instrument's carrying amount.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: How is impairment of financial assets at amortised cost measured under IFRS 9?
options: Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk
At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)

When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: IFRS 9 requires an expected credit loss model. For assets that have not had a significant increase in credit risk since initial recognition, 12-month ECL is recognised. For those that have, lifetime ECL is recognised.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: When is a financial liability measured at amortised cost under IFRS 9?
options: Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial liabilities are generally measured at amortised cost using the effective interest method, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss (e.g. to eliminate an accounting mismatch).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: What is a financial instrument under IFRS 9?
options: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial instrument is any contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity. It includes primary instruments (e.g. receivables, debt) and derivatives.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Can an entity designate a financial asset at fair value through profit or loss to eliminate an accounting mismatch?
options: Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Yes, but only for equity instruments that the entity intends to hold for the short term as part of an actively managed trading portfolio subject to frequent buying and selling decisions

Yes; the fair value through profit or loss designation is available in all circumstances where management considers it produces more relevant and decision-useful information for users of the financial statements

No; the fair value through profit or loss measurement category is reserved exclusively for derivative financial instruments and assets held within a trading portfolio under IFRS 9 classification rules
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: An entity may designate a financial asset at fair value through profit or loss at initial recognition if this eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency (an 'accounting mismatch') that would otherwise arise from measuring assets and liabilities on different bases.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: How are equity instruments (e.g. shares in other entities) generally measured under IFRS 9?
options: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Investments in equity instruments are generally measured at fair value through profit or loss. An irrevocable election may be made at initial recognition to present changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (with no recycling to profit or loss on derecognition).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: When is a financial asset derecognised under IFRS 9?
options: Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk

When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)

A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is derecognised when (a) the contractual rights to the cash flows expire, or (b) the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. the entity has transferred substantially all risks and rewards of ownership).

options: At amortised cost using the effective interest method

When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is measured at amortised cost if (1) it is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and (2) its contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest (SPPI). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 14: What is the effective interest method under IFRS 9?
options: When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding

A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency

When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: The effective interest method is a method of calculating the amortised cost of a financial asset or liability and of allocating the interest income or expense over the relevant period. The effective interest rate is the rate that exactly discounts estimated future cash flows to the instrument's carrying amount. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 15: How is impairment of financial assets at amortised cost measured under IFRS 9?
options: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk

When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: IFRS 9 requires an expected credit loss model. For assets that have not had a significant increase in credit risk since initial recognition, 12-month ECL is recognised. For those that have, lifetime ECL is recognised. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 16: When is a financial liability measured at amortised cost under IFRS 9?
options: Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial liabilities are generally measured at amortised cost using the effective interest method, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss (e.g. to eliminate an accounting mismatch). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 17: What is a financial instrument under IFRS 9?
options: Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial instrument is any contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity. It includes primary instruments (e.g. receivables, debt) and derivatives. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 18: Can an entity designate a financial asset at fair value through profit or loss to eliminate an accounting mismatch?
options: No; the fair value through profit or loss measurement category is reserved exclusively for derivative financial instruments and assets held within a trading portfolio under IFRS 9 classification rules

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Yes, but only for equity instruments that the entity intends to hold for the short term as part of an actively managed trading portfolio subject to frequent buying and selling decisions

Yes; the fair value through profit or loss designation is available in all circumstances where management considers it produces more relevant and decision-useful information for users of the financial statements
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: An entity may designate a financial asset at fair value through profit or loss at initial recognition if this eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency (an 'accounting mismatch') that would otherwise arise from measuring assets and liabilities on different bases. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 19: How are equity instruments (e.g. shares in other entities) generally measured under IFRS 9?
options: Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk
At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Investments in equity instruments are generally measured at fair value through profit or loss. An irrevocable election may be made at initial recognition to present changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (with no recycling to profit or loss on derecognition). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 20: When is a financial asset derecognised under IFRS 9?
options: When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)
Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk

A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount

Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is derecognised when (a) the contractual rights to the cash flows expire, or (b) the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. the entity has transferred substantially all risks and rewards of ownership). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Revision check 21: Under IFRS 9, how are debt instruments measured when held in the amortised cost category? Choose the best answer.
options: A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.correct: At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial assets measured at amortised cost are initially recognised at fair value and subsequently at amortised cost using the effective interest method, less any impairment. The business model must be to hold to collect contractual cash flows. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Under IAS 21, at what exchange rate should a foreign currency transaction be initially recorded?
options: The forward rate applicable to the settlement date of the transaction as quoted by the entity's bank
The closing rate at the end of the reporting period, applied consistently to all foreign currency transactions
The spot rate at the date of the transaction
The average rate for the reporting period, calculated as the arithmetic mean of daily spot rates
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: The spot rate at the date of the transaction
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: IAS 21 requires a foreign currency transaction to be recorded, on initial recognition, in the functional currency by applying to the foreign currency amount the spot exchange rate between the functional currency and the foreign currency at the date of the transaction.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: What is the functional currency under IAS 21?
options: The currency in which the financial statements are presented
The currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates
At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: The currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: The functional currency is the currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates (i.e. the environment in which it generates and spends cash).

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: What is the presentation currency under IAS 21?
options: The spot rate at the date of the transaction
At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
The currency in which the financial statements are presented
At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: The currency in which the financial statements are presented
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: The presentation currency is the currency in which the financial statements are presented. It may be different from the functional currency.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: At what rate should foreign currency monetary items be translated at the reporting date under IAS 21?
options: The currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates
At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
The currency in which the financial statements are presented
At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: At the end of each reporting period, foreign currency monetary items are translated using the closing rate. Non-monetary items carried at historical cost are translated at the transaction date rate.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Where are foreign exchange differences on monetary items recognised under IAS 21?
options: In profit or loss (except when the gain or loss is part of the net investment in a foreign operation and the item is accounted for as equity)
Yes; if there is a change in the underlying transactions, events and conditions, the functional currency may change. The change is applied prospectively
In other comprehensive income (and accumulated in a separate component of equity) until disposal of the net investment
The financial statements are first restated in accordance with IAS 29 (if the functional currency is hyperinflationary), then translated using the closing rate
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: In profit or loss (except when the gain or loss is part of the net investment in a foreign operation and the item is accounted for as equity)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Exchange differences arising on the translation of monetary items (or on the settlement of monetary items) are generally recognised in profit or loss in the period in which they arise. Exceptions apply for certain net investment in a foreign operation and for hedge accounting.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: How are non-monetary items carried at fair value in a foreign currency translated under IAS 21?
options: At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
The spot rate at the date of the transaction
At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
The currency in which the financial statements are presented
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: A non-monetary item measured at fair value in a foreign currency is translated using the exchange rate at the date when the fair value was determined. This produces the carrying amount in the functional currency.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: How should the results and financial position of a foreign operation be translated for inclusion in the entity's financial statements under IAS 21?
options: The financial statements are first restated in accordance with IAS 29 (if the functional currency is hyperinflationary), then translated using the closing rate
In profit or loss (except when the gain or loss is part of the net investment in a foreign operation and the item is accounted for as equity)
Assets and liabilities at the closing rate; income and expenses at the exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate); equity at historical rates
Yes; if there is a change in the underlying transactions, events and conditions, the functional currency may change. The change is applied prospectively
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.correct: Assets and liabilities at the closing rate; income and expenses at the exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate); equity at historical rates
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Assets and liabilities of the foreign operation are translated at the closing rate. Income and expenses are translated at exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate). Equity components are translated at historical rates. Exchange differences are recognised in OCI.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Where are exchange differences arising on the translation of a foreign operation recognised when the operation is consolidated?
options: The currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates
Yes; if there is a change in the underlying transactions, events and conditions, the functional currency may change. The change is applied prospectively
In other comprehensive income (and accumulated in a separate component of equity) until disposal of the net investment
In profit or loss (except when the gain or loss is part of the net investment in a foreign operation and the item is accounted for as equity)

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Under IAS 33, how is basic earnings per share calculated?
options: Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
Profit after tax divided by number of shares at year end
Profit divided by number of shares at the start of the period
Operating profit divided by issued shares
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Basic EPS = (Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders) / (Weighted average number of ordinary shares outstanding during the period).

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: What is the numerator (profit) for basic EPS under IAS 33?
options: Total comprehensive income attributable to equity holders, including items recognised in other comprehensive income
Operating profit for the period, excluding interest income, interest expense, and the income tax charge
Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Profit before tax for the period, as reported in the consolidated statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The numerator is profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent. It is after tax and after deducting preference dividends (and other appropriations in respect of preference shares) for the period.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: How is the weighted average number of ordinary shares calculated when new shares are issued for cash during the period?
options: If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Using the treasury share method: assume proceeds from exercise are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price; the net increase in shares is added to the denominator
Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The weighted average number of ordinary shares during the period is the number of shares outstanding at the beginning of the period, adjusted by shares issued or cancelled during the period, weighted by the number of days they were outstanding (or a reasonable approximation).

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: What is a dilutive potential ordinary share under IAS 33?
options: A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
An equity instrument that is subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments (i.e. carries the residual interest)
Basic and diluted EPS for the current period and for any comparative periods presented are restated
Yes; an entity presents basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: A dilutive potential ordinary share is one that would decrease profit per share (or increase loss per share) from continuing operations. Only dilutive instruments are included in diluted EPS.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: How is diluted EPS calculated under IAS 33?
options: Operating profit for the period excluding interest income, interest expense on borrowings, and the full income tax charge, as this represents the earnings generated from core trading activities available to all capital providers

Profit before tax for the reporting period as presented on the face of the consolidated statement of profit or loss, before deducting the income tax expense and before adjusting for any potential dilutive instruments outstanding

Total comprehensive income attributable to ordinary equity holders including all items of other comprehensive income, with no adjustment for dilutive potential ordinary shares because the denominator-only treasury share method is applied

Profit attributable to ordinary equity holders plus the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares, divided by the weighted average number of ordinary shares plus the weighted average number of shares that would be issued on conversion/exercise of dilutive potential ordinary shares
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Profit attributable to ordinary equity holders plus the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares, divided by the weighted average number of ordinary shares plus the weighted average number of shares that would be issued on conversion/exercise of dilutive potential ordinary shares
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Diluted EPS adjusts the numerator for the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares and the denominator for the number of additional ordinary shares that would be issued if dilutive instruments were converted or exercised.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: When are contingently issuable shares included in diluted EPS?
options: Contingently issuable shares are included in diluted EPS only when legally issued by the entity; they cannot be treated as outstanding before the actual date of issue and registration in the share register
If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)

Contingently issuable shares are included in basic EPS from the date the contingency condition is met; they are included in diluted EPS only in a subsequent period after confirmation by the entity's auditors

Contingently issuable shares are always excluded from both basic and diluted EPS calculations until actually issued, regardless of whether the conditions for issuance have been satisfied by the end of the reporting period
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Contingently issuable ordinary shares are included in diluted EPS from the beginning of the period (or from the date of the contingent share agreement) if the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the reporting period.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: How are share options and warrants typically reflected in diluted EPS?
options: If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Using the treasury share method: assume proceeds from exercise are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price; the net increase in shares is added to the denominator
The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Using the treasury share method: assume proceeds from exercise are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price; the net increase in shares is added to the denominator
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The treasury share method assumes that the proceeds from exercise of options/warrants are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price during the period. The net increase in the number of shares (shares that would be issued less shares deemed repurchased) is dilutive and is added to the denominator.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Does IAS 33 require disclosure of basic and diluted EPS for profit from continuing operations?
options: Yes; an entity presents basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders
If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Yes; an entity presents basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: IAS 33 requires presentation of basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent. EPS may also be presented for other line items (e.g. discontinued operations).

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: What is an ordinary share under IAS 33?
options: An equity instrument that is subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments (i.e. carries the residual interest)
A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
Basic and diluted EPS for the current period and for any comparative periods presented are restated
Yes; an entity presents basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: An equity instrument that is subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments (i.e. carries the residual interest)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Ordinary shares are equity instruments that are subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments. They participate in profit for the period only after other types of shares (e.g. preference shares) have been allocated.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: When comparative figures are restated (e.g. for a prior period error), how should EPS be presented?
options: Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
An equity instrument that is subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments (i.e. carries the residual interest)
Basic and diluted EPS for the current period and for any comparative periods presented are restated
A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Basic and diluted EPS for the current period and for any comparative periods presented are restated
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: If the number of ordinary or potential ordinary shares outstanding changes as a result of a capitalisation, bonus issue or share split, or a reverse share split, basic and diluted EPS for all periods presented are adjusted retrospectively.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 33, how is basic earnings per share calculated?
options: Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
Profit after tax divided by number of shares at year end
Operating profit divided by issued shares
Profit divided by number of shares at the start of the period
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Basic EPS = (Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders) / (Weighted average number of ordinary shares outstanding during the period). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Exam practice 12: What is the numerator (profit) for basic EPS under IAS 33?
options: Profit before tax for the period, as reported in the consolidated statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income
Total comprehensive income attributable to equity holders, including items recognised in other comprehensive income
Operating profit for the period, excluding interest income, interest expense, and the income tax charge
Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The numerator is profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent. It is after tax and after deducting preference dividends (and other appropriations in respect of preference shares) for the period. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Exam practice 13: How is the weighted average number of ordinary shares calculated when new shares are issued for cash during the period?
options: Using the treasury share method: assume proceeds from exercise are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price; the net increase in shares is added to the denominator
Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The weighted average number of ordinary shares during the period is the number of shares outstanding at the beginning of the period, adjusted by shares issued or cancelled during the period, weighted by the number of days they were outstanding (or a reasonable approximation). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Exam practice 14: What is a dilutive potential ordinary share under IAS 33?
options: Basic and diluted EPS for the current period and for any comparative periods presented are restated
Yes; an entity presents basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders
A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
An equity instrument that is subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments (i.e. carries the residual interest)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: A dilutive potential ordinary share is one that would decrease profit per share (or increase loss per share) from continuing operations. Only dilutive instruments are included in diluted EPS. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Exam practice 15: How is diluted EPS calculated under IAS 33?
options: Profit before tax for the reporting period as presented on the face of the consolidated statement of profit or loss, before deducting the income tax expense and before adjusting for any potential dilutive instruments outstanding

Total comprehensive income attributable to ordinary equity holders including all items of other comprehensive income, with no adjustment for dilutive potential ordinary shares because the denominator-only treasury share method is applied

Profit attributable to ordinary equity holders plus the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares, divided by the weighted average number of ordinary shares plus the weighted average number of shares that would be issued on conversion/exercise of dilutive potential ordinary shares

Operating profit for the period excluding interest income, interest expense on borrowings, and the full income tax charge, as this represents the earnings generated from core trading activities available to all capital providers
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: Profit attributable to ordinary equity holders plus the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares, divided by the weighted average number of ordinary shares plus the weighted average number of shares that would be issued on conversion/exercise of dilutive potential ordinary shares
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Diluted EPS adjusts the numerator for the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares and the denominator for the number of additional ordinary shares that would be issued if dilutive instruments were converted or exercised. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Exam practice 16: When are contingently issuable shares included in diluted EPS?
options: Contingently issuable shares are always excluded from both basic and diluted EPS calculations until actually issued, regardless of whether the conditions for issuance have been satisfied by the end of the reporting period

Contingently issuable shares are included in diluted EPS only when legally issued by the entity; they cannot be treated as outstanding before the actual date of issue and registration in the share register
If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)

Contingently issuable shares are included in basic EPS from the date the contingency condition is met; they are included in diluted EPS only in a subsequent period after confirmation by the entity's auditors
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.correct: If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Contingently issuable ordinary shares are included in diluted EPS from the beginning of the period (or from the date of the contingent share agreement) if the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the reporting period. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: Does IFRS 10 require consolidation of all subsidiaries?
options: Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)

Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months

A parent consolidates all subsidiaries except when the parent is itself a subsidiary and its other owners do not object, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale and meets the criteria

Combining the financial statements of the parent and its subsidiaries line by line; adding like items; eliminating intra-group balances and transactions; and eliminating the parent's investment and the subsidiary's equity
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: A parent consolidates all subsidiaries except when the parent is itself a subsidiary and its other owners do not object, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale and meets the criteria
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: A parent consolidates all subsidiaries. Exemptions exist when the parent is a subsidiary and its non-controlling owners have been informed and do not object to not receiving consolidated financial statements, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale (measured at fair value through profit or loss).

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: What is the consolidation process under IFRS 10?
options: Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)

A parent consolidates all subsidiaries except when the parent is itself a subsidiary and its other owners do not object, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale and meets the criteria

Combining the financial statements of the parent and its subsidiaries line by line; adding like items; eliminating intra-group balances and transactions; and eliminating the parent's investment and the subsidiary's equity

Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: Combining the financial statements of the parent and its subsidiaries line by line; adding like items; eliminating intra-group balances and transactions; and eliminating the parent's investment and the subsidiary's equity
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: Consolidation combines like items of assets, liabilities, equity, income and expenses. The parent's investment in the subsidiary and the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity are eliminated. Intra-group balances, transactions, income and expenses are eliminated.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: Can a parent and its subsidiary have different reporting dates under IFRS 10?
options: Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months
