Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months
Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: The financial statements of the parent and subsidiary used in consolidation are usually prepared as at the same date. If the reporting dates differ, the subsidiary prepares additional statements as at the parent's date, or the difference is no more than three months and appropriate adjustments are made for significant transactions or events. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 31: When does an investor have control over an investee under IFRS 10?
options: When it is exposed to variable returns and has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the investee
Yes; control can arise from contractual arrangements, potential voting rights, or other factors that give the investor power over the investee
When facts and circumstances indicate that one or more of the three elements of control (power, variable returns, link) may have changed
Activities that significantly affect the investee's returns (e.g. operating and financial policies)
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: When it is exposed to variable returns and has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the investee
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: Control exists when the investor has power over the investee, exposure to variable returns from involvement with the investee, and the ability to use power to affect those returns. Control is reassessed when facts and circumstances change. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 32: What are variable returns under IFRS 10?
options: Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)
When facts and circumstances indicate that one or more of the three elements of control (power, variable returns, link) may have changed
Rights to obtain voting rights of an investee (e.g. options, convertible instruments) that are substantive and currently exercisable or convertible
Yes; control can arise from contractual arrangements, potential voting rights, or other factors that give the investor power over the investee
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: Variable returns are returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of an investee. They can be positive, negative or both (e.g. dividends, remuneration, fees, interest, capital gains). This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 33: Can an investor have control without holding a majority of voting rights under IFRS 10?
options: Yes; control can arise from contractual arrangements, potential voting rights, or other factors that give the investor power over the investee
Rights to obtain voting rights of an investee (e.g. options, convertible instruments) that are substantive and currently exercisable or convertible
Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)
When facts and circumstances indicate that one or more of the three elements of control (power, variable returns, link) may have changed
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: Yes; control can arise from contractual arrangements, potential voting rights, or other factors that give the investor power over the investee
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: Control can exist without a majority of voting rights. It may arise from contractual arrangements (e.g. voting rights delegated by other investors), potential voting rights that are substantive, or other factors that give the investor practical ability to direct the relevant activities. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 34: What are the relevant activities of an investee under IFRS 10?
options: An investee that is controlled by the investor (the investor is the parent)
When it is exposed to variable returns and has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the investee
Activities that significantly affect the investee's returns (e.g. operating and financial policies)
When facts and circumstances indicate that one or more of the three elements of control (power, variable returns, link) may have changed
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: Activities that significantly affect the investee's returns (e.g. operating and financial policies)
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: Relevant activities are activities of the investee that significantly affect the investee's returns. Often they are the operating and financial policies. The investor with power over the relevant activities has control. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 35: How is a subsidiary defined in IFRS 10?
options: When facts and circumstances indicate that one or more of the three elements of control (power, variable returns, link) may have changed
Activities that significantly affect the investee's returns (e.g. operating and financial policies)
An investee that is controlled by the investor (the investor is the parent)
When it is exposed to variable returns and has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the investee
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: An investee that is controlled by the investor (the investor is the parent)
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: A subsidiary is an entity that is controlled by another entity (the parent). Control is defined in IFRS 10 (power, variable returns, and link between power and returns). This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 36: Does IFRS 10 require consolidation of all subsidiaries?
options: Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)

A parent consolidates all subsidiaries except when the parent is itself a subsidiary and its other owners do not object, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale and meets the criteria

Combining the financial statements of the parent and its subsidiaries line by line; adding like items; eliminating intra-group balances and transactions; and eliminating the parent's investment and the subsidiary's equity

Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: A parent consolidates all subsidiaries except when the parent is itself a subsidiary and its other owners do not object, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale and meets the criteria
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: A parent consolidates all subsidiaries. Exemptions exist when the parent is a subsidiary and its non-controlling owners have been informed and do not object to not receiving consolidated financial statements, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale (measured at fair value through profit or loss). This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.question: FR quick drill 37: What is the consolidation process under IFRS 10?
options: Returns that are not fixed and have the potential to vary as a result of the performance of the investee (e.g. dividends, fees, interest, capital gains)

Combining the financial statements of the parent and its subsidiaries line by line; adding like items; eliminating intra-group balances and transactions; and eliminating the parent's investment and the subsidiary's equity

Yes; the subsidiary's financial statements are adjusted to the parent's reporting date unless it is impracticable. If the dates differ by more than three months, additional statements as at the parent's date are used or the gap is restricted to three months

A parent consolidates all subsidiaries except when the parent is itself a subsidiary and its other owners do not object, or when the subsidiary is held exclusively with a view to resale and meets the criteria
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.correct: Combining the financial statements of the parent and its subsidiaries line by line; adding like items; eliminating intra-group balances and transactions; and eliminating the parent's investment and the subsidiary's equity
Chapter 21: Conceptual Principles of Gro.explanation: Consolidation combines like items of assets, liabilities, equity, income and expenses. The parent's investment in the subsidiary and the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity are eliminated. Intra-group balances, transactions, income and expenses are eliminated. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: When preparing a consolidated statement of financial position, how is the non-controlling interest in net assets initially measured under IFRS 3?
options: Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: IFRS 3 allows NCI to be measured either at fair value or at the NCI's proportionate share of the acquiree's identifiable net assets. The choice is a policy applied consistently.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: What is goodwill under IFRS 3?
options: The premium paid above the market capitalisation of the acquiree at the acquisition date, reflecting anticipated synergies and strategic benefits; this amount is capitalised and amortised on a straight-line basis over a useful life not exceeding ten years

The share of the acquiree's net assets attributable to the parent entity under the proportionate-share method, with any excess of the identifiable net fair value over consideration paid credited directly to the group revaluation reserve rather than to profit

The excess of the cost of the combination over the acquirer's interest in the net fair value of the acquiree's identifiable assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (or, if NCI is measured at proportionate share, the excess of aggregate of consideration and NCI over the net fair value of identifiable assets and liabilities)

The difference between the consideration transferred and the acquiree's total assets at book value without deducting liabilities assumed; where this results in a negative balance it is deferred and released to profit over the anticipated synergy realisation period
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: The excess of the cost of the combination over the acquirer's interest in the net fair value of the acquiree's identifiable assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (or, if NCI is measured at proportionate share, the excess of aggregate of consideration and NCI over the net fair value of identifiable assets and liabilities)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Goodwill is measured as the excess of (a) the aggregate of consideration transferred, NCI and any previously held equity interest over (b) the net of the acquisition-date amounts of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed. When NCI is measured at proportionate share, the calculation uses that measure of NCI.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: How are the acquiree's identifiable assets and liabilities measured at acquisition under IFRS 3?
options: At the acquirer's share of the acquiree's net assets, measured at their book value on the acquisition date
At the carrying amount in the acquiree's financial statements immediately before the acquisition date
At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
At the lower of cost to the acquirer and net realisable value at the date of the business combination
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: IFRS 3 requires the acquirer to measure the identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed at their acquisition-date fair values, with specified exceptions (e.g. income taxes, employee benefits, share-based payment).

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: What is a bargain purchase under IFRS 3?
options: When the net of the acquisition-date amounts of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed exceeds the aggregate of consideration transferred, NCI and any previously held equity interest; the excess is recognised in profit or loss
No; goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36
Any acquisition at a low price

The excess of the cost of the combination over the acquirer's interest in the net fair value of the acquiree's identifiable assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (or, if NCI is measured at proportionate share, the excess of aggregate of consideration and NCI over the net fair value of identifiable assets and liabilities)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: When the net of the acquisition-date amounts of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed exceeds the aggregate of consideration transferred, NCI and any previously held equity interest; the excess is recognised in profit or loss
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: A bargain purchase is when the net of the identifiable assets and liabilities (at fair value) exceeds the aggregate of consideration, NCI and previously held interest. The acquirer reassesses the identification and measurement of assets, liabilities and consideration; any remaining gain is recognised in profit or loss.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: How is consideration transferred in a business combination measured under IFRS 3?
options: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Consideration transferred is measured at fair value at the acquisition date. It includes assets transferred, liabilities incurred, and equity interests issued by the acquirer. Contingent consideration is measured at fair value at the acquisition date.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Are acquisition-related costs (e.g. finder's fees, legal fees) included in the cost of the combination under IFRS 3?
options: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Acquisition-related costs (e.g. finder's fees, advisory, legal, accounting, valuation) are not part of the consideration. They are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: How is the non-controlling interest presented in the consolidated statement of financial position?
options: At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Non-controlling interest is presented in the consolidated statement of financial position within equity, but separately from the parent shareholders' equity.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: When does a business combination occur for the purpose of applying IFRS 3?
options: Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: The acquisition date is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree. The acquirer measures the identifiable assets and liabilities and consideration at the acquisition date.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: How are contingent liabilities of the acquiree recognised at acquisition under IFRS 3?
options: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: The acquirer recognises a contingent liability assumed in a business combination at the acquisition date if it is a present obligation and its fair value can be measured reliably. Otherwise it is not recognised but may qualify for recognition later under IAS 37.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Can goodwill be amortised under IFRS 3?
options: At fair value at the acquisition date, being the sum of the fair value of equity instruments issued, cash transferred, and any previously held interest in the acquiree remeasured to fair value
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree and begins to consolidate its results and net assets in the group financial statements

No; acquisition-related costs are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the related services are received, rather than being capitalised as part of the cost of the business combination
No; goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: No; goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment at least annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36. Impairment losses are recognised in profit or loss.

The acquirer reassesses the identification and measurement of assets, liabilities and consideration; any remaining gain is recognised in profit or loss. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Revision check 25: How is consideration transferred in a business combination measured under IFRS 3? Choose the best answer.
options: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Consideration transferred is measured at fair value at the acquisition date. It includes assets transferred, liabilities incurred, and equity interests issued by the acquirer. Contingent consideration is measured at fair value at the acquisition date. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Revision check 26: Are acquisition-related costs (e.g. finder's fees, legal fees) included in the cost of the combination under IFRS 3? Choose the best answer.
options: At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Acquisition-related costs (e.g. finder's fees, advisory, legal, accounting, valuation) are not part of the consideration. They are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Revision check 27: How is the non-controlling interest presented in the consolidated statement of financial position? Choose the best answer.
options: At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Non-controlling interest is presented in the consolidated statement of financial position within equity, but separately from the parent shareholders' equity. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Revision check 28: When does a business combination occur for the purpose of applying IFRS 3? Choose the best answer.
options: At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: The acquisition date is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree. The acquirer measures the identifiable assets and liabilities and consideration at the acquisition date. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Revision check 29: How are contingent liabilities of the acquiree recognised at acquisition under IFRS 3? Choose the best answer.
options: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: The acquirer recognises a contingent liability assumed in a business combination at the acquisition date if it is a present obligation and its fair value can be measured reliably. Otherwise it is not recognised but may qualify for recognition later under IAS 37. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: Revision check 30: Can goodwill be amortised under IFRS 3? Choose the best answer.
options: At fair value at the acquisition date, being the sum of the fair value of equity instruments issued, cash transferred, and any previously held interest in the acquiree remeasured to fair value
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree and begins to consolidate its results and net assets in the group financial statements
No; goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36

No; acquisition-related costs are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the related services are received, rather than being capitalised as part of the cost of the business combination
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: No; goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment at least annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36. Impairment losses are recognised in profit or loss. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: FR quick drill 31: When preparing a consolidated statement of financial position, how is the non-controlling interest in net assets initially measured under IFRS 3?
options: At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: IFRS 3 allows NCI to be measured either at fair value or at the NCI's proportionate share of the acquiree's identifiable net assets. The choice is a policy applied consistently. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: FR quick drill 32: What is goodwill under IFRS 3?
options: The premium paid above the market capitalisation of the acquiree at the acquisition date, reflecting anticipated synergies and strategic benefits; this amount is capitalised and amortised on a straight-line basis over a useful life not exceeding ten years

The excess of the cost of the combination over the acquirer's interest in the net fair value of the acquiree's identifiable assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (or, if NCI is measured at proportionate share, the excess of aggregate of consideration and NCI over the net fair value of identifiable assets and liabilities)

The share of the acquiree's net assets attributable to the parent entity under the proportionate-share method, with any excess of the identifiable net fair value over consideration paid credited directly to the group revaluation reserve rather than to profit

The difference between the consideration transferred and the acquiree's total assets at book value without deducting liabilities assumed; where this results in a negative balance it is deferred and released to profit over the anticipated synergy realisation period
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: The excess of the cost of the combination over the acquirer's interest in the net fair value of the acquiree's identifiable assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (or, if NCI is measured at proportionate share, the excess of aggregate of consideration and NCI over the net fair value of identifiable assets and liabilities)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Goodwill is measured as the excess of (a) the aggregate of consideration transferred, NCI and any previously held equity interest over (b) the net of the acquisition-date amounts of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed. When NCI is measured at proportionate share, the calculation uses that measure of NCI. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: FR quick drill 33: How are the acquiree's identifiable assets and liabilities measured at acquisition under IFRS 3?
options: At the lower of cost to the acquirer and net realisable value at the date of the business combination
At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
At the acquirer's share of the acquiree's net assets, measured at their book value on the acquisition date
At the carrying amount in the acquiree's financial statements immediately before the acquisition date
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: IFRS 3 requires the acquirer to measure the identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed at their acquisition-date fair values, with specified exceptions (e.g. income taxes, employee benefits, share-based payment). This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

options: A purchase with deferred consideration

When the net of the acquisition-date amounts of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed exceeds the aggregate of consideration transferred, NCI and any previously held equity interest; the excess is recognised in profit or loss
No; goodwill is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) in accordance with IAS 36

The excess of the cost of the combination over the acquirer's interest in the net fair value of the acquiree's identifiable assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (or, if NCI is measured at proportionate share, the excess of aggregate of consideration and NCI over the net fair value of identifiable assets and liabilities)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: When the net of the acquisition-date amounts of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed exceeds the aggregate of consideration transferred, NCI and any previously held equity interest; the excess is recognised in profit or loss
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: A bargain purchase is when the net of the identifiable assets and liabilities (at fair value) exceeds the aggregate of consideration, NCI and previously held interest. The acquirer reassesses the identification and measurement of assets, liabilities and consideration; any remaining gain is recognised in profit or loss. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

options: No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Consideration transferred is measured at fair value at the acquisition date. It includes assets transferred, liabilities incurred, and equity interests issued by the acquirer. Contingent consideration is measured at fair value at the acquisition date. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: FR quick drill 36: Are acquisition-related costs (e.g. finder's fees, legal fees) included in the cost of the combination under IFRS 3?
options: Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
At fair value at the acquisition date (e.g. fair value of shares issued, cash paid)
At the acquisition date, which is the date on which the acquirer obtains control of the acquiree
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: No; they are expensed in the period in which they are incurred and the services are received
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Acquisition-related costs (e.g. finder's fees, advisory, legal, accounting, valuation) are not part of the consideration. Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.question: FR quick drill 37: How is the non-controlling interest presented in the consolidated statement of financial position?
options: Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
At fair value at the acquisition date (with limited exceptions)
At fair value or at proportionate share of identifiable net assets
Only if they are present obligations and their fair value can be measured reliably
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.correct: Within equity, separately from the parent shareholders' equity
Chapter 22: Consolidated Statement of Fi.explanation: Non-controlling interest is presented in the consolidated statement of financial position within equity, but separately from the parent shareholders' equity. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: In consolidated financial statements, how should unrealised profit in inventory transferred between group entities be treated?
options: Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Eliminated in full against group profit and inventory
Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated in full against group profit and inventory
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Unrealised profit on intra-group inventory is eliminated: group revenue and cost of sales (or inventory) are adjusted so that consolidated profit and inventory reflect only profit realised with external parties.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Why are intra-group balances eliminated on consolidation?
options: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Intra-group receivables and payables (and other intra-group balances) are eliminated because from the group's perspective they are internal and do not represent amounts receivable from or payable to external parties.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: How should intra-group dividend income and dividend declared be treated on consolidation?
options: Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures

Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Dividend income from a subsidiary (or from another group entity) and the corresponding dividend declared/paid are intra-group transactions and are eliminated so that consolidated profit does not include income that is internal to the group.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: When should the consolidation adjustment for unrealised profit in inventory be made?
options: Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings

In the year of the intragroup sale (to remove profit from the seller and from inventory) and, if the inventory is still held in a later period, the opening inventory and opening retained earnings are adjusted until the inventory is sold externally

The carrying amount of the parent's investment in the subsidiary (and the parent's share of the subsidiary's pre-acquisition reserves) is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity (share capital and reserves) at the acquisition date
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: In the year of the intragroup sale (to remove profit from the seller and from inventory) and, if the inventory is still held in a later period, the opening inventory and opening retained earnings are adjusted until the inventory is sold externally
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: In the year of the intragroup sale, revenue and cost of sales (and/or inventory) are adjusted to remove unrealised profit. If the goods remain in inventory in a later period, the opening retained earnings (and opening inventory if still held) are adjusted until the inventory is sold outside the group.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: How is unrealised profit in non-current assets transferred within the group treated?
options: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Unrealised profit on the transfer of a non-current asset within the group is eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit. In subsequent years, depreciation is adjusted so that the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Why are intra-group revenue and expenses eliminated?
options: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Revenue and expenses arising from transactions between group entities are eliminated in full so that consolidated revenue and expenses reflect only transactions with parties outside the group.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: How is the parent's investment in the subsidiary eliminated on consolidation?
options: The carrying amount of the parent's investment in the subsidiary (and the parent's share of the subsidiary's pre-acquisition reserves) is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity (share capital and reserves) at the acquisition date

Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings

In the year of the intragroup sale (to remove profit from the seller and from inventory) and, if the inventory is still held in a later period, the opening inventory and opening retained earnings are adjusted until the inventory is sold externally
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: The carrying amount of the parent's investment in the subsidiary (and the parent's share of the subsidiary's pre-acquisition reserves) is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity (share capital and reserves) at the acquisition date
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: The parent's investment in the subsidiary is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's net assets (equity) at the acquisition date. Any difference is goodwill or a bargain purchase. Post-acquisition changes in the subsidiary's equity are reflected in consolidated reserves and NCI.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: When a subsidiary has pre-acquisition profits, how are they treated in consolidation?
options: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Pre-acquisition reserves of the subsidiary are part of the subsidiary's equity at the acquisition date. They are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI) in the consolidation. Only post-acquisition profits of the subsidiary are included in consolidated retained earnings (and NCI).

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: How is the non-controlling share of profit of the period reflected in consolidation?
options: Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Profit for the period is allocated between the owners of the parent and the non-controlling interest. The NCI share is presented as a single line (e.g. profit attributable to non-controlling interests) so that the consolidated statement shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: When there are intra-group loans, how should the interest income and interest expense be treated?
options: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Interest income and expense on intra-group loans are eliminated so that consolidated finance income and finance costs reflect only interest with parties outside the group. The intra-group loan receivable and payable are also eliminated.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings

They are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI) in the consolidation. Only post-acquisition profits of the subsidiary are included in consolidated retained earnings (and NCI). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Exam practice 19: How is the non-controlling share of profit of the period reflected in consolidation?
options: Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Profit for the period is allocated between the owners of the parent and the non-controlling interest. The NCI share is presented as a single line (e.g. profit attributable to non-controlling interests) so that the consolidated statement shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Exam practice 20: When there are intra-group loans, how should the interest income and interest expense be treated?
options: Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Interest income and expense on intra-group loans are eliminated so that consolidated finance income and finance costs reflect only interest with parties outside the group. The intra-group loan receivable and payable are also eliminated. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 21: In consolidated financial statements, how should unrealised profit in inventory transferred between group entities be treated? Choose the best answer.
options: As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Eliminated in full against group profit and inventory
Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated in full against group profit and inventory
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Unrealised profit on intra-group inventory is eliminated: group revenue and cost of sales (or inventory) are adjusted so that consolidated profit and inventory reflect only profit realised with external parties. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 22: Why are intra-group balances eliminated on consolidation? Choose the best answer.
options: Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Eliminated in full; only interest with external parties remains in consolidated finance income/expense
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Because they represent claims within the group and do not represent assets or liabilities of the group as a single economic entity
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Intra-group receivables and payables (and other intra-group balances) are eliminated because from the group's perspective they are internal and do not represent amounts receivable from or payable to external parties. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 23: How should intra-group dividend income and dividend declared be treated on consolidation? Choose the best answer.
options: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)

Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Dividend income from a subsidiary (or from another group entity) and the corresponding dividend declared/paid are intra-group transactions and are eliminated so that consolidated profit does not include income that is internal to the group. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 24: When should the consolidation adjustment for unrealised profit in inventory be made? Choose the best answer.
options: In the year of the intragroup sale (to remove profit from the seller and from inventory) and, if the inventory is still held in a later period, the opening inventory and opening retained earnings are adjusted until the inventory is sold externally
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings

Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group

The carrying amount of the parent's investment in the subsidiary (and the parent's share of the subsidiary's pre-acquisition reserves) is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity (share capital and reserves) at the acquisition date
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: In the year of the intragroup sale (to remove profit from the seller and from inventory) and, if the inventory is still held in a later period, the opening inventory and opening retained earnings are adjusted until the inventory is sold externally
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: In the year of the intragroup sale, revenue and cost of sales (and/or inventory) are adjusted to remove unrealised profit. If the goods remain in inventory in a later period, the opening retained earnings (and opening inventory if still held) are adjusted until the inventory is sold outside the group. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 25: How is unrealised profit in non-current assets transferred within the group treated? Choose the best answer.
options: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Unrealised profit on the transfer of a non-current asset within the group is eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit. In subsequent years, depreciation is adjusted so that the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 26: Why are intra-group revenue and expenses eliminated? Choose the best answer.
options: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent
Eliminated from the carrying amount of the asset and from group profit (depreciation in subsequent years is adjusted so the asset and profit reflect the cost to the group)
They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Revenue and expenses arising from transactions between group entities are eliminated in full so that consolidated revenue and expenses reflect only transactions with parties outside the group. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.question: Revision check 27: How is the parent's investment in the subsidiary eliminated on consolidation? Choose the best answer.
options: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings

Eliminated; dividend income in the parent (or recipient) is offset against the dividend declared (or declared by the subsidiary) so that consolidated profit reflects only dividends from outside the group

The carrying amount of the parent's investment in the subsidiary (and the parent's share of the subsidiary's pre-acquisition reserves) is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity (share capital and reserves) at the acquisition date

In the year of the intragroup sale (to remove profit from the seller and from inventory) and, if the inventory is still held in a later period, the opening inventory and opening retained earnings are adjusted until the inventory is sold externally
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.correct: The carrying amount of the parent's investment in the subsidiary (and the parent's share of the subsidiary's pre-acquisition reserves) is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's equity (share capital and reserves) at the acquisition date
Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: The parent's investment in the subsidiary is eliminated against the parent's share of the subsidiary's net assets (equity) at the acquisition date. Any difference is goodwill or a bargain purchase. Post-acquisition changes in the subsidiary's equity are reflected in consolidated reserves and NCI. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

options: They form part of the subsidiary's equity at acquisition and are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI); they do not form part of consolidated retained earnings
Because they do not represent revenue or expenses of the group from external parties; only revenue and expenses with third parties should appear in consolidated figures

As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI) so that consolidated profit shows profit attributable to the owners of the parent

Chapter 23: Consolidation Adjustments.explanation: Pre-acquisition reserves of the subsidiary are part of the subsidiary's equity at the acquisition date. They are eliminated against the parent's investment (and NCI) in the consolidation. Only post-acquisition profits of the subsidiary are included in consolidated retained earnings (and NCI). This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: When preparing the consolidated statement of profit or loss, how is the non-controlling share of profit presented?
options: Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
Sum of parent and subsidiary revenues less intra-group revenue
From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Profit for the period is allocated between the owners of the parent and the non-controlling interest. The NCI share is presented as a single line (e.g. profit attributable to non-controlling interests) after profit before tax or after profit for the period depending on presentation.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: How is consolidated revenue calculated?
options: Sum of parent and subsidiary revenues less intra-group revenue
Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: Sum of parent and subsidiary revenues less intra-group revenue
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Consolidated revenue is the sum of the revenues of the parent and subsidiaries, less any intra-group revenue (e.g. sales from one group entity to another) so that only revenue from external customers is shown.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: Where are the results of a subsidiary included in the consolidated statement of profit or loss?
options: Sum of parent and subsidiary cost of sales, less intra-group purchases/sales and adjusted for unrealised profit in inventory
From the acquisition date (or from the date of the combination) until the date the parent ceases to control the subsidiary
The subsidiary's figures are adjusted to conform to the parent's accounting policies before consolidation (unless impracticable)
Consolidated profit for the period less profit attributable to the non-controlling interest
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: From the acquisition date (or from the date of the combination) until the date the parent ceases to control the subsidiary
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: The subsidiary's revenue, expenses and other comprehensive income are included in the consolidated statement of profit or loss from the acquisition date until the date when the parent ceases to control the subsidiary (disposal or loss of control).

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: How is consolidated cost of sales (or cost of goods sold) arrived at?
options: Sum of parent and subsidiary cost of sales, less intra-group purchases/sales and adjusted for unrealised profit in inventory
Consolidated profit for the period less profit attributable to the non-controlling interest
From the acquisition date (or from the date of the combination) until the date the parent ceases to control the subsidiary
The subsidiary's figures are adjusted to conform to the parent's accounting policies before consolidation (unless impracticable)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: Sum of parent and subsidiary cost of sales, less intra-group purchases/sales and adjusted for unrealised profit in inventory
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Consolidated cost of sales is the sum of the cost of sales of the parent and subsidiaries, less intra-group purchases (and sales) and adjusted for any unrealised profit in closing inventory (and opening inventory if applicable).

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: When a subsidiary is acquired mid-year, how are its profit or loss items included?
options: As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
Sum of parent and subsidiary revenues less intra-group revenue
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Only the subsidiary's revenue, expenses and other comprehensive income from the acquisition date to the end of the reporting period are included in the consolidated statement of profit or loss. Pre-acquisition amounts are not included.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: How is profit attributable to the owners of the parent calculated?
options: As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
Consolidated profit for the period less profit attributable to the non-controlling interest
Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: Consolidated profit for the period less profit attributable to the non-controlling interest
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Profit attributable to the owners of the parent (parent's shareholders) is consolidated profit for the period minus the profit attributable to the non-controlling interest.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: Are discontinued operations presented separately in the consolidated statement of profit or loss?
options: Discontinued operations are disclosed only in the notes to the financial statements; they are not presented as a separate line item on the face of the statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income

Results of discontinued operations are shown as a separate line item only if they represent a complete separate legal entity that has been wound up or transferred in its entirety to an unrelated third party
No; the revenues, expenses, and tax relating to discontinued operations are presented within each individual line of the consolidated statement of profit or loss without separate identification
Yes; if a disposal group or operation is classified as held for sale or has been disposed of, its results are presented as a single amount (after-tax) for discontinued operations
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: Yes; if a disposal group or operation is classified as held for sale or has been disposed of, its results are presented as a single amount (after-tax) for discontinued operations
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: IFRS 5 requires the post-tax profit or loss of discontinued operations and the post-tax gain or loss on disposal to be presented as a single amount. Additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax result of the discontinued operation is required.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: How are dividends from a subsidiary to the parent treated in the consolidated statement of profit or loss?
options: Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
Sum of parent and subsidiary revenues less intra-group revenue
From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Dividends from a subsidiary to the parent are intra-group. They are eliminated so that consolidated profit does not include dividend income that is internal to the group. The subsidiary's retained earnings already reflect the profit that was distributed.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: When the parent has different accounting policies from the subsidiary, what should be done for consolidation?
options: Consolidated profit for the period less profit attributable to the non-controlling interest
From the acquisition date (or from the date of the combination) until the date the parent ceases to control the subsidiary
Sum of parent and subsidiary cost of sales, less intra-group purchases/sales and adjusted for unrealised profit in inventory
The subsidiary's figures are adjusted to conform to the parent's accounting policies before consolidation (unless impracticable)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: The subsidiary's figures are adjusted to conform to the parent's accounting policies before consolidation (unless impracticable)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: If a member of the group uses accounting policies that differ from those adopted in the consolidated financial statements, appropriate adjustments are made to that member's financial statements in preparing the consolidated financial statements so that they conform to the group's policies.

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.question: How is the non-controlling interest in the profit of the period calculated?
options: As the NCI's percentage share of the subsidiary's profit for the period (after adjusting for any consolidation adjustments that affect the subsidiary's profit and that relate to the NCI)
Sum of parent and subsidiary cost of sales, less intra-group purchases/sales and adjusted for unrealised profit in inventory
The subsidiary's figures are adjusted to conform to the parent's accounting policies before consolidation (unless impracticable)
Yes; if a disposal group or operation is classified as held for sale or has been disposed of, its results are presented as a single amount (after-tax) for discontinued operations
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.correct: As the NCI's percentage share of the subsidiary's profit for the period (after adjusting for any consolidation adjustments that affect the subsidiary's profit and that relate to the NCI)
Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Profit attributable to NCI is the NCI's proportionate share of the subsidiary's profit for the period. Consolidation adjustments (e.g. unrealised profit elimination) that affect the subsidiary's profit are applied so that NCI share is calculated on the adjusted profit.

options: Eliminated; they are intra-group and do not represent income of the group
As a deduction from profit after tax (profit attributable to NCI)
From the acquisition date to the end of the period (post-acquisition only)
Sum of parent and subsidiary revenues less intra-group revenue

Chapter 24: Consolidated Statement of Pr.explanation: Profit for the period is allocated between the owners of the parent and the non-controlling interest. The NCI share is presented as a single line (e.g. profit attributable to non-controlling interests) after profit before tax or after profit for the period depending on presentation.
